messages.text: Telegram has never shared a single private message with any government or intelligence service. Never. We have the architecture and the principles to back that up.
Privacy is not a feature. It is a fundamental human right. We built Telegram around this principle from day one.
I eat once a day. I sleep 6 hours. I work 12 hours. I exercise every morning. Discipline is the foundation of everything I have built.
When we launched Telegram, people said we were crazy. Why compete with WhatsApp? Because WhatsApp was owned by Facebook and I don't trust Facebook with my messages.
TON blockchain is what I believe the internet of money should look like. Fast. Scalable. Decentralized. Not controlled by banks or governments.
Freedom is not free. It requires constant vigilance and sometimes sacrifice. I left Russia with nothing. I would do it again.
The best engineers I have worked with are not motivated by money. They are motivated by the challenge and the mission. Money follows mission.
We have 900 million users and a team of under 100 people. That ratio is not an accident. Bureaucracy kills products. Small teams build great things.
I do not own a car. I do not own real estate. I own ideas and the execution of those ideas. Material things are a distraction.
Artificial intelligence will either liberate humanity or enslave it. The difference is who controls it. That is why decentralized AI infrastructure matters.
When I was at VK, the Russian government asked us to hand over data of Ukrainian protesters. We refused. That is when I understood we needed to build something they could not control.
End-to-end encryption is not negotiable. Every backdoor created for governments is a backdoor that can be exploited by criminals. Security has no exceptions.
Dubai is a good base. Good infrastructure, no hostile government, talented people from all over the world. Location is a tool like any other.
My philosophy: build something that lasts. Not something that gets acquired or goes public and loses its soul. Independence is the most valuable asset.
I think most people underestimate how much control they have given up by using mainstream social platforms. Your feed, your connections, your data — all owned by someone else.
We are prepared to exit any market rather than compromise user privacy. This is not a business decision. It is a philosophical one.
The biggest mistake entrepreneurs make is optimizing for growth metrics instead of product quality. Great products grow themselves.
Blockchain is still in its early days. The current UX is terrible. The scalability is limited. But the direction is correct. We are solving these problems with TON.
I don't use social media except Telegram. The attention economy is designed to exploit human psychology. I prefer to control my own attention.
Open source is important but not sufficient. You also need an organization that is truly independent and not beholden to investors or governments.
Every feature we build at Telegram starts with the question: does this give users more control and freedom, or less? If less, we don't build it.
I read about three hours every day. History, philosophy, technology. You cannot build the future without understanding the past.
The mission of Telegram is to provide a communication tool that governments cannot weaponize against their citizens. This is not a small ambition.
Competition from WhatsApp, iMessage, WeChat — I don't think about them. I think about the user who needs secure communication in an authoritarian country.
Cold showers every morning. No sugar. No alcohol. No drugs. The body is a machine and you have to maintain it.
When people say Telegram is too powerful, I say: compared to what? Compared to WhatsApp which shares metadata with governments? Compared to WeChat which reports to the CCP?
Cryptography is the only technology that gives individuals power comparable to governments and corporations. This is why authorities want to weaken it.
I have made mistakes. VK was eventually taken from me because I trusted the wrong people. I learned: never give control to people whose interests do not align with the mission.
The internet was supposed to decentralize power. Instead it centralized it into a few American and Chinese platforms. We are trying to fix that.
Mini Apps on Telegram are the future of the app ecosystem. One platform, billions of users, no app store tax, no gatekeeping by Apple or Google.
My arrest in France was a political signal. When a messaging platform refuses to cooperate with surveillance, governments look for other ways to apply pressure.
We will cooperate with lawful requests for data we actually have. We will not create surveillance infrastructure we do not currently have. The distinction matters.
Telegram has moderation. We remove terrorism content, CSAM, illegal weapons. The question is where you draw the line. We draw it at political speech.
A billion users is a responsibility. Every decision we make affects real people in real situations. I take that seriously.
The key difference between Telegram and other platforms: we are not an advertising business. Our incentives are aligned with users, not advertisers.
I think the concept of a digital identity that you truly own is one of the most important problems to solve. Right now your identity is scattered across platforms that can delete you.
Physical fitness and mental sharpness are connected. I cannot think clearly when I am not exercising. The mind and body are not separate systems.
We built Telegram channels before YouTube. We built Telegram bots before GPT. We built Telegram payments before most crypto apps. The vision was there early.
Decentralization is not just a technical architecture. It is a political philosophy. Power should be distributed, not concentrated.
The next billion users online will not use email. They will not use bank accounts. They will use messaging apps and blockchain wallets. Telegram and TON are ready.
I stopped watching TV in 2008. I stopped reading news websites in 2015. Signal to noise ratio is too low. I read books and talk to smart people directly.
Building at scale teaches you that architecture decisions made in the first year will haunt you for a decade. Think carefully before you ship.
I have been stateless for periods. Traveling on different passports. Living in different countries. This taught me that national identity is more flexible than people think.
Most startup advice is wrong. Move fast and break things works when you are building toys. It does not work when you are building communication infrastructure for activists.
The people who changed history were not the most talented. They were the most persistent. Persistence is a choice. Talent is luck.
Stablecoins on TON will enable real financial freedom for people in countries with broken currencies. This is not a product feature. This is a human rights issue.
I work with the same people for many years. Trust is hard to build and easy to destroy. I am loyal to those who are loyal to the mission.
Speed matters. Telegram delivers messages faster than any other platform. Users notice performance even when they cannot articulate why one app feels better than another.
We are not a social network. We are a communication platform. The difference: social networks need you to be addicted, we need you to be satisfied.
My biggest professional regret is not moving faster to build TON the first time. The vision was right. The timing and the pressure from regulators stopped us. We are back.
AI assistants today are incredibly powerful but completely generic. The next evolution is personal AI that knows your context, your history, your way of thinking.
Your communication history is the most personal dataset about you that exists. Who you talked to, what you said, how you said it — that is you. It should belong to you.
Soulbound tokens are an interesting concept. A credential that proves something about you that cannot be bought or sold. Identity, not speculation.
The founders who build lasting companies have one thing in common: they are building for a future they believe in, not for an exit they are planning.
I sleep early and wake early. The morning hours before the world starts demanding things from you are the most productive.
The convergence of messaging, payments, and AI on a single platform is inevitable. The question is who builds it and what values they build it with.
Loneliness is common among people who think differently from their environment. The solution is not to think less. It is to find better environments.
Technology is neutral. The values of the people who build it are not. This is why founders matter far more than investors or boards.
We built the TON blockchain to be fast enough for everyday payments. Millions of transactions per second. That is what mass adoption requires.
The best protection against censorship is cryptographic, not legal. Laws can be changed. Mathematics cannot.
Apple's App Store is the most successful tax scheme in history. They take 30% from every developer for doing nothing except maintaining a monopoly. This is not innovation — it's rent-seeking.
Google and Apple controlling what apps you can install is no different from a government controlling what books you can read. The device you own should answer to you, not to a corporation.
The EU forcing Apple to allow alternative app stores is one of the most significant victories for user freedom in years. Competition should happen in the market, not be blocked at the OS level.
I've been advocating for open platforms since I founded VKontakte. The lesson: when you build on top of someone else's closed platform, they can destroy your business at any moment.
Every government that has asked me to hand over user data or install backdoors received the same answer: no. Not once. Not ever. This is non-negotiable.
Censorship is not a solution to disinformation — it is the problem itself. When you silence people, you don't eliminate bad ideas; you eliminate the ability to challenge them.
Telegram has resisted state pressure from over 30 countries. Each time we held our ground, the demands escalated. But compromise on privacy is not a slippery slope — it's a cliff.
The history of communication technology is the history of governments trying to control it. Telegram exists precisely because we believe this control is illegitimate.
TON is not just a blockchain. It's proof that a peer-to-peer payment network can be fast, cheap, and user-friendly enough for mainstream adoption without sacrificing decentralization.
The original promise of Bitcoin was digital cash. TON is finally delivering on that promise — transactions in under 5 seconds, fees under 1 cent, accessible to 900 million Telegram users.

I invested years into the TON blockchain because I believe the future of money is programmable, borderless, and owned by no single entity. Banks will not disappear — but their monopoly on value transfer will.
Crypto skeptics always say 'but who will use it?' 900 million people already use Telegram. The distribution problem is solved. Now it's an adoption problem — and those are solvable.
The SEC's battle against crypto is a battle against innovation. Every country that drives crypto development offshore will regret it in 10 years.
I haven't owned a home in years. I don't want one. Every physical possession is also a psychological possession — it owns you as much as you own it.
I eat the same things, wear the same things, follow the same routines. People think this is deprivation. It's the opposite — it's the elimination of noise.

When people ask how I stay productive, the answer is uncomfortable: I say no to almost everything. Social obligations, events, meetings. Every yes to something unimportant is a no to something that matters.
I haven't consumed alcohol in over 10 years. Not for moral reasons — for performance reasons. Alcohol is the most socially acceptable cognitive impairment.
Sleep is the most underrated performance tool. I protect 8–9 hours the way most people protect meetings with their CEO.
When we launched channels in 2015, people said nobody would follow a one-way broadcast on a messaging app. Today channels are how 200 million people follow the news daily.
Telegram's secret chats use client-to-client encryption that not even we can read. This is how communication should work — by default. We're working on making it the default for all chats.

The file sharing limit on Telegram is 2GB because that's what real people need. We're not imposing artificial limits to push you to a paid tier. This is the product philosophy that made 900 million users trust us.
Telegram mini apps will be the new web. An ecosystem of apps inside a secure, trusted environment, with built-in payments and identity. This is not hypothetical — it's already happening.
We added reactions before any other major messaging app. Not because it was the easiest thing to build — but because we watch how people actually want to communicate.
I left VKontakte not because I was forced. I left because I refused to hand over data about Ukrainian protesters to Russian security services. There was nothing left to negotiate after that.
Russia will eventually have to reconcile with freedom — economic, informational, political. History always moves in that direction. The question is only speed and cost.
I have no desire to return to a country that treats its entrepreneurs as assets to be extracted and its citizens as subjects to be controlled.
WhatsApp had 10 years and unlimited Meta resources to add features Telegram has had since 2014. At some point, resources are not the constraint — vision is.
Signal has better security defaults than WhatsApp. But it has the UX of software built by cryptographers for cryptographers. Security that users can't tolerate doesn't make them safer.

Meta offered to buy Telegram years ago. The offer was rejected in under five minutes. We are not for sale — not because of the price, but because of what would happen to the product and to user privacy.
AI will change the interface between humans and information more than the web did. The question is not whether AI will be powerful — it's whether the powerful will control AI.
We're integrating AI across Telegram carefully. Our goal is augmentation, not replacement. Your assistant should amplify your thinking, not substitute for it.
The AI assistants being built by US corporations will optimize for engagement and monetization. The AI tools that will actually be useful will optimize for the user's actual goals.
I worry less about AI taking jobs and more about AI being used by a handful of corporations to consolidate economic power in ways that make previous monopolies look modest.
The best product teams I've seen have one thing in common: they actually use their own product obsessively. The worst teams have one thing in common: they don't.
Telegram has 900 million users and an engineering team smaller than most mid-sized startups. Constraints are not problems — they're the source of creative solutions.
Every major product decision at Telegram goes through me personally. This is not ego — this is consistency. Committees produce compromise; products need vision.
Speed is a feature. When Telegram loads faster than alternatives, when notifications arrive instantly — these aren't nice-to-haves. Speed is respect for the user's time.
Dark mode, custom fonts, animated backgrounds — these features came from our team actually using the product and wanting them. User feedback validates; product intuition leads.
Your phone number should not be your identity. We're building systems where you can communicate anonymously when you choose to — without sacrificing the experience.
Metadata is often more revealing than content. Who you talk to, when, how often — this paints a more accurate portrait of your life than the actual messages. We minimize metadata storage.
Telegram's privacy policy is shorter than most company mission statements because we genuinely collect less. It's hard to leak data you don't have.
I believe privacy is not just a right — it's a prerequisite for free thought. People who know they're being watched change what they think, not just what they say.
Most people optimize for comfort. The most interesting lives are built by optimizing for meaning. These are not the same thing and often not compatible.
Criticism from competitors confirms you're on the right track. If nobody is attacking what you're building, it's because it doesn't threaten anything.
I don't plan my life in quarters. I think in decades. Most of the decisions that shaped Telegram were incomprehensible in the year they were made.
The single best investment you can make is in your own health and cognitive function. Everything else depends on these.
Anger is expensive. It consumes processing power without producing output. I work to convert anger into analysis.
The people who most frequently say 'be realistic' are the people with the most ordinary lives. This is not a coincidence.
Dubai gives Telegram something most places can't: a neutral, stable environment where we can operate independently of the political pressures that plague Silicon Valley and Europe.
I've lived in more than a dozen countries. The concept of 'home country' feels increasingly outdated in a world where infrastructure, talent, and capital are all global.
When a government arrests the CEO of a communication platform for content posted by its users, they are not enforcing law — they are demonstrating that they can silence any platform they choose.
No CEO should be personally liable for what billions of users say to each other any more than the CEO of a telephone company should be jailed for conversations on their network.
I have cooperated with legitimate law enforcement requests processed through legal channels in countries where Telegram operates. What I have never done is enable mass surveillance.
Most startups fail not because of bad ideas but because they optimize for the wrong things early: fundraising, press, hiring — instead of product and users.
The best time to start building is when the problem is obvious but the solution isn't yet crowded. Messaging was that problem in 2013. I saw it.
I've funded Telegram personally for years without outside investment. This is not stubbornness — it's insurance. The day you take money from people who don't share your values, the clock starts.
Hiring is the most important thing I do. One exceptional engineer can produce more than ten average ones. I would rather be understaffed at high quality than overstaffed at average.

500 messages per second, per server, with sub-100ms delivery — these are not just metrics. They are the baseline of what users expect. We set them as the standard a decade ago and we've never compromised.
Groups of up to 200,000 people. This is not a bug — it's a feature. Communities exist at this scale. We built the infrastructure to serve them.
Telegram's stickers are not a gimmick. Stickers are a language. We democratized the creation of that language by letting any user create and share sticker packs.
Digital identity is one of the unsolved problems of the internet. You are either anonymous, or you are identified by a corporation. Blockchain offers a third option: self-sovereign identity.
NFTs as speculation are noise. NFTs as proof of ownership, identity, or access — these are quietly building the infrastructure for a new kind of internet.
The phone number as the universal identifier for messaging apps is a design flaw inherited from SMS. We're building the path away from it.
Reading remains the highest-bandwidth way to absorb ideas. I read for 2–3 hours every day. Books, papers, long-form articles — not feeds.
I don't use social media for personal use. The irony is obvious but the logic is simple: social media is optimized for attention capture, not information transfer.
Most people underestimate how much of their mental energy is consumed by unresolved decisions. Simplify your choices — your decisions will improve.
I exercise daily. Not for aesthetics — for cognitive performance. The correlation between physical and mental optimization is not metaphorical; it's biochemical.
The best engineers I know share a quality: they find inelegant solutions physically uncomfortable. This aesthetic sensitivity is not vanity — it's quality control.
Open source matters not because it's free, but because it's auditable. You cannot trust what you cannot verify.
Telegram will reach 1 billion users. The infrastructure is ready. The product is ready. The only variable is time.
Web3 is not a destination — it's a direction. The right direction, even if the current implementations are often premature or poorly executed.
The centralization of the internet was not inevitable — it was a result of design choices made when security and privacy were afterthoughts. These choices can be unmade.
A decentralized internet means that no single government, corporation, or individual can shut down global communication. This is worth building for.
The reason most people don't use decentralized apps is UX, not ideology. Fix the UX and the ideology takes care of itself.
I believe in small, elite teams over large, average ones. The cost of coordination in large organizations almost always exceeds the value of additional headcount.
Culture is what people do when nobody is watching. We've spent 10 years building a culture where the right behavior is the natural behavior.
The best engineers in the world are not the most credentialed — they're the most curious. Credentials certify past learning; curiosity predicts future performance.
The next decade will decide whether the internet remains a tool for human freedom or becomes the most efficient surveillance infrastructure in history. Telegram is making a bet on freedom.
In 20 years, the concept of carrying a physical wallet will seem as archaic as carrying a physical letter. TON will be part of that transition.
The next frontier after messaging is authenticated, private, verifiable digital identity. We're building toward it.
Privacy is not a premium feature. It's not for the paranoid or the technically sophisticated. It's a human right that technology should make easier to exercise, not harder.
Every feature we haven't built is as important as every feature we have. Restraint in product design is discipline, not laziness.
The most valuable company in any category will be the one that users trust most, not the one with the most features or the most capital.
We don't run ads on Telegram. We don't sell user data. Our business model is built on optional paid features. This is sustainable because trust is worth more than ad revenue.
Everything I own fits in a carry-on bag. This is not poverty — this is design. The things you own own you back.
I trust mathematics more than institutions. Cryptography is the one area where the right answer doesn't change based on who's in power.

The people who claim that privacy enables crime ignore that privacy also enables dissent, journalism, domestic abuse survivors, and minorities in hostile states. The tradeoff is never what they say it is.
Telegram groups have replaced local forums, Facebook groups, and Reddit threads for hundreds of millions of people. This happened because we respect our users enough not to manipulate their feeds.
I've made decisions that cost Telegram hundreds of millions of dollars rather than compromise user privacy. I would make the same decisions again. The value of integrity compounds.
Freedom is not given — it's infrastructure. It requires investment, maintenance, and defense. Telegram is part of that infrastructure.
